text: We describe in this video the G-pouch microvascularization assessment using the knee infrared technology.
We will perform three different assessments.
The first one after we're building the pouch, the second after the elongation procedure if needed, and the final product at the end of the surgery after the G-pouch has been performed.
The surgeon uses a near-infrared scope to visualize the surgical field, followed by the intravenous injection of three milliliters of endocyanin green to assess microvascularization.
The screen displays the signal from the near-infrared scope, indicating the presence of endocyanin green within the microvascular structures.
As you can see on that picture, there is a tree square on the left.
The first one is a normal view.
The video displays a black and white near-infrared view, showing the surgical field under enhanced imaging conditions.
And the third one is an enhanced reality view.
it is easily possible to change the large screen according to the different screen on the left.
The confection of a G-pouch after coloprotectomy is of major importance for the future function of the pouch.
Therefore, we think it is valuable to have a look at the microvascularization of the pouch.
The surgeon frees the small bowel mesentery to maximize its length, preparing to examine the microvascularization of the pouch.
The surgeon clamps or ligates some of the vascular supply of the pouch to obtain sufficient length for the anastomosis during the elongation procedure.
The surgeon uses a vascular clamp to occlude the vascular supply, visibly restricting blood flow to the targeted area.
We want to be absolutely sure that the blood supply reaches the bottom end of the pouch.
You can see on the left of the screen three squares.
The screen displays three visual modes: normal light, infrared light, and enhanced reality, each providing different visual perspectives of the surgical field.
Under black and white light, the surgeon demonstrates that the vascular supply to the pouch remains intact following the elongation procedure.
The surgeon divides the vessels using surgical instruments, following the clamping of the vascular supply.
The surgeon positions the pouch at the bottom end in preparation for the anastomosis, ensuring it is safely aligned for the procedure.
The surgeon checks the anastomosis from the outside using an intra-abdominal view, with the joint visible at the top end of the surgical field.
The surgeon injects endocyanin, causing the vessels and microvessels to fluoresce brightly under infrared light, clearly visible up to the level of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an endoanal view of the G-pouch, inspecting the interior for any signs of bleeding and assessing the appearance of the pouch lining.
The surgeon assesses the vascularization of the surgical site before proceeding with the creation of an ileostomy.
The surgeon inspects the stable line of the anastomosis, clearly visible in the intra-abdominal view, and identifies any bleeding sites for precise localization and stitching.
If everything is fine you can finish the operation in a very safe manner.
In conclusion, the use of an ear infrared scope in GPOW surgery is highly feasible and adds safety about the patch blood supply at the time of surgery.
